q: A company needs to grant temporary access to an S3 bucket for a third-party auditor without creating an IAM user. What is the MOST secure approach?
o: Make the S3 bucket public temporarily
Use an IAM role with an external ID and cross-account access
Create an IAM user with time-limited credentials
e: Cross-account IAM roles with external IDs prevent confused deputy attacks. Temporary credentials, no permanent keys shared.

q: An application on EC2 needs to read objects from S3. What is the MOST secure way to provide access?
o: Create an IAM user and store keys in a config file
Embed access keys in the application code
Store access keys in environment variables
Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance with S3 read permissions
e: IAM roles for EC2 provide temporary credentials via instance metadata. No long-term keys to manage or rotate.

q: A company must encrypt all data at rest in S3 with minimal operational overhead. Which option should they use?
e: SSE-S3: AWS manages keys entirely. Zero operational overhead. Each object encrypted with unique key.

q: A solutions architect needs to ensure an ALB only accepts traffic from CloudFront. What should they configure?
o: Security group allowing only the CloudFront managed prefix list
e: CloudFront managed prefix list in ALB security group ensures only CloudFront can reach the ALB.

q: An application stores database credentials that must be rotated automatically every 30 days. Which service meets this?
o: AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation
e: Secrets Manager: built-in automatic rotation with Lambda. Supports RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB natively.

q: A company needs to restrict S3 bucket access to requests originating only from their VPC. Which approach?
o: IAM policy attached to all users
S3 bucket policy with aws:SourceVpc condition
e: S3 bucket policy with aws:SourceVpc condition restricts access to traffic from specific VPC/endpoint.

q: A company wants to prevent any IAM user from disabling CloudTrail. What is the BEST approach?
o: Use an SCP in AWS Organizations to deny cloudtrail:StopLogging
e: SCP: organization-wide guardrail. Deny StopLogging action. Even admins cannot override.

q: A web application is experiencing SQL injection attacks. Which AWS service protects against this?
o: AWS WAF with SQL injection match rules
e: WAF: HTTP layer protection. SQL injection match conditions inspect request body/URI.

q: A solutions architect needs web servers publicly accessible but databases unreachable from internet. What architecture?
o: All resources in one public subnet
Web servers in public subnet, databases in private subnet
e: Multi-tier: public subnet (web/ALB) + private subnet (DB, no IGW route). NAT for outbound only.

q: A company needs a private connection between VPC and S3 without traversing public internet. What should they use?
e: Gateway Endpoint: free, private path to S3. Traffic stays on AWS network. No NAT/IGW needed.

q: A company wants to detect when an S3 bucket is made public accidentally. Which service?
e: AWS Config managed rules check compliance continuously. Alerts on public buckets.

q: A company needs single sign-on for multiple AWS accounts. Which service?
e: IAM Identity Center: central SSO for multiple accounts. Integrates with AD, SAML IdPs.

q: A company stores sensitive PII in S3 and needs automatic discovery and classification. Which service?
e: Macie: ML-powered discovery of PII, financial data in S3. Classifies sensitivity level.

q: Which service provides dedicated single-tenant HSMs for cryptographic key storage?
e: CloudHSM: dedicated HSM appliances in your VPC. Single-tenant, FIPS 140-2 Level 3.

q: An application needs to authenticate mobile users and provide temporary AWS credentials. Which service?
o: IAM users for each mobile user
e: Cognito: user pools for auth, identity pools for temp AWS credentials. Scales to millions.

q: A company needs VPC-to-VPC connectivity across multiple VPCs without complex peering. What should they use?
e: Transit Gateway: hub-and-spoke. Connect hundreds of VPCs. Single gateway. Simplifies routing.

q: EC2 instances in a private subnet need to download patches but cannot be reached from internet. What is needed?
o: NAT Gateway in a public subnet with route from private subnet
Internet Gateway attached to private subnet
e: NAT Gateway: outbound internet for private subnet. Instances initiate out. No inbound from internet.

q: A company needs to manage TLS certificates for their ALB with auto-renewal. Which service?
e: ACM: free public certificates. Auto-renewal. Integrates with ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway.

q: A company wants to block all public access to all S3 buckets account-wide. Simplest approach?
o: Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level
e: S3 Block Public Access at account level: overrides any bucket policy or ACL granting public access.

q: A company needs to ensure all EBS volumes are encrypted by default. What should they enable?
o: EBS encryption by default setting in EC2 console for the region
e: EBS encryption by default: account-level setting per region. All new volumes auto-encrypted.

q: A web application must remain available if an entire AZ fails. What is the MINIMUM architecture?
o: EC2 instances in at least two AZs behind an ALB with Auto Scaling
e: Multi-AZ: ALB + Auto Scaling across 2+ AZs. One AZ fails, traffic routes to healthy instances.

q: A stateless app has unpredictable traffic spikes. What ensures availability and cost efficiency?
o: Auto Scaling group with target tracking scaling policy behind ALB
e: Auto Scaling + target tracking: adjusts capacity to maintain target metric. Pay only for what you use.

q: A company needs their RDS database to survive an AZ failure with automatic failover. What should they enable?
e: RDS Multi-AZ: synchronous standby in another AZ. Automatic failover in 60-120 seconds.

q: Messages in SQS fail processing. They should be investigated later without being lost. What to configure?
o: Configure a dead-letter queue (DLQ) with maxReceiveCount
e: DLQ: after maxReceiveCount failures, SQS moves message to DLQ for debugging.

q: Application must continue during an entire Region outage. What pattern?
o: Multi-Region active-active or active-passive with Route 53 failover
e: Multi-Region: deploy in 2+ Regions. Route 53 failover. Data replication across Regions.

q: A solutions architect needs to decouple a web tier from a processing tier that takes minutes per request. What to use?
o: SQS queue between web tier and processing tier
e: SQS: decouples components. Web sends message, processing polls at its own pace.

q: On-premises app with 4-hour RTO and 1-hour RPO for DR on AWS. Most cost-effective strategy?
e: Pilot light: minimal core running (DB replication). Scale up on disaster. Meets 4hr RTO, 1hr RPO.

q: System must automatically replace unhealthy EC2 instances. What combination?
o: Auto Scaling group with ELB health checks
e: ASG + ELB health checks: unhealthy instance terminated, replacement launched automatically.

q: Company needs to replicate S3 objects to another Region for compliance and DR. Which feature?
e: CRR: automatic async replication to another Region. Versioning required on both buckets.

q: Aurora MySQL needs read scalability and automatic failover. What to configure?
o: Aurora cluster with read replicas across multiple AZs
e: Aurora cluster: writer + up to 15 read replicas across AZs. Auto failover in under 30 seconds.

q: Stateful app stores sessions locally on EC2. Users lose session on failover. How to fix?
o: Externalize session state to ElastiCache Redis or DynamoDB
e: Externalize state: any instance serves any user. Enables scaling and failover without session loss.

q: DynamoDB table needs low latency in both us-east-1 and eu-west-1. What to implement?
e: Global Tables: multi-Region, multi-active. Auto-replication. Read/write in any Region.

q: Application must failover automatically between two Regions. Which Route 53 policy?
o: Failover routing policy with health checks
e: Failover routing: active-passive. Health checks monitor primary. Unhealthy triggers failover.

q: Order processing must happen exactly once, in order. Which SQS feature?
o: SQS FIFO queue with message deduplication
e: FIFO queue: exactly-once processing, strict ordering. Deduplication built-in.

q: S3 protection against accidental deletion. Which TWO features? (Select TWO)
e: Versioning preserves all versions. MFA Delete requires MFA to permanently delete.

q: Serverless application processes S3 uploads. What event-driven architecture?
e: S3 events -> Lambda: fully serverless. No servers. Scales automatically.

q: Currently daily EBS snapshots (24hr RPO). Need 1-hour RPO. What to change?
o: Hourly EBS snapshots using Data Lifecycle Manager
e: Hourly snapshots via Data Lifecycle Manager. Meets 1-hour RPO. Automated.

q: Legacy monolith needs AWS migration with minimal changes plus improved availability. What approach?
o: Rehost on EC2 with Multi-AZ Auto Scaling behind ALB
e: Rehost: lift and shift. Add Multi-AZ + ASG + ALB. Minimal code changes.

q: Highly available message broker needed for MQTT and AMQP protocols. Which service?
e: Amazon MQ: managed ActiveMQ/RabbitMQ. Supports MQTT, AMQP. Multi-AZ.

q: Critical DynamoDB data needs point-in-time recovery. What to enable?
e: PITR: continuous backups. Restore to any second within 35-day window.

q: Company needs to deliver static content globally with low latency. Which service?
e: CloudFront: CDN caches at 400+ edge locations. S3 as origin. Lowest latency for static content.

q: Sub-millisecond read latency needed for DynamoDB handling millions of requests/sec. What to add?
e: DAX: in-memory cache for DynamoDB. Microsecond reads. Compatible with DynamoDB API.

q: Real-time dashboard needs to ingest clickstream data from millions of users. Which service?
e: Kinesis Data Streams: real-time ingestion at millions of records/sec. Low latency.

q: In-memory cache needed supporting complex data structures (sorted sets, lists). Which service?
e: ElastiCache Redis: sorted sets, lists, hashes. Persistence. Replication. Pub/sub.

q: Company needs to transfer 50 TB to S3 quickly. Internet is 1 Gbps. What to use?
e: 50TB over 1Gbps = ~5 days. Snowball Edge: physical device, ship to AWS. Faster for large datasets.

q: IoT application handles millions of time-series data points per second. Which database?
e: Timestream: purpose-built time-series DB. Serverless, auto-scales.

q: Ultra-low latency TCP traffic handling needed. ALB or NLB?
e: NLB: Layer 4, millions of requests/sec, ultra-low latency. Static IPs. Best for TCP/UDP.

q: Redshift queries on historical data spanning years are slow. What to implement?
o: Redshift Spectrum to query data in S3
e: Redshift Spectrum: query S3 without loading. Keep recent in Redshift, archive to S3.

q: Transaction-heavy database needs consistent high IOPS. Which EBS volume type?
e: io2 Block Express: up to 256,000 IOPS, 99.999% durability. For mission-critical databases.

q: HPC workloads require high network throughput between instances. What to use?
e: Cluster Placement Group + EFA: OS-bypass networking, 100 Gbps. Best for HPC.

q: S3 bucket receives 10,000 PUT/s. How to maximize throughput?
e: S3: 3,500 PUT/s per prefix. Multiple prefixes = multiplied throughput.

q: Containerized app with variable traffic needs cost optimization. What compute platform?
e: Fargate: serverless containers. Pay per vCPU/memory per second. No idle capacity cost.

q: Accelerate S3 uploads from global users. Which feature?
e: Transfer Acceleration: uses edge locations for upload. Faster long-distance via AWS network.

q: Shared file system needed across multiple EC2 instances in different AZs. Which service?
e: EFS: NFS file system. Shared across instances and AZs. Auto-scales.

q: Batch processing tolerates interruptions. Lowest compute cost?
e: Spot: up to 90% off. Fault-tolerant workloads handle interruptions.

q: Social network app needs complex relationship queries. Which database?
e: Neptune: graph DB. Optimized for relationship traversal. Gremlin and SPARQL.

q: Lambda cold starts causing latency for critical API. What to configure?
e: Provisioned Concurrency: pre-initialized environments. Eliminates cold starts.

q: E-commerce product catalog needs full-text search. Which service?
e: OpenSearch: full-text search, faceting, autocomplete, relevance ranking.

q: Reduce database load for app that repeatedly reads same data. What caching layer?
e: ElastiCache: in-memory cache. Check cache first, fall back to DB. Reduces read load 80-90%.

q: Windows file share accessible from multiple EC2 instances needed. Which service?
o: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
e: FSx for Windows: SMB protocol, AD integration, DFS. Multi-AZ option.

q: EC2 runs 24/7 steady-state for 3 years. Most savings?
o: 3-year All Upfront Reserved Instances or Compute Savings Plan
e: 3-year All Upfront: up to 72% off On-Demand. Predictable workload = commit.

q: Dev environments run only 8 AM-6 PM. How to reduce costs?
o: Instance Scheduler to stop/start outside business hours
e: Instance Scheduler: stop at 6 PM, start at 8 AM. Pay 10 hours instead of 24.

q: 100 TB logs in S3 Standard. After 30 days rarely accessed, retrievable within 12 hours. How to optimize?
o: Lifecycle policy to Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 30 days
e: Lifecycle to Glacier Flexible Retrieval: retrieval in 3-5 hours. ~80% cheaper than Standard.

q: High data transfer costs between VPC and S3. How to eliminate?
o: Use a VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3
e: Gateway Endpoint: free. No NAT Gateway data processing charges. No transfer charges.

q: Unpredictable DynamoDB traffic from 0 to 50,000 reads/sec. Most cost-effective capacity mode?
e: On-Demand: pay per request. No capacity planning. No charge when idle.

q: Identify unused or underutilized EC2 instances. Which service?
o: AWS Compute Optimizer and Cost Explorer right-sizing
e: Compute Optimizer: analyzes utilization. Recommends right-size or terminate.

q: Fault-tolerant batch workload can handle interruptions. Cheapest compute?
e: Spot: up to 90% off. Fault-tolerant handles 2-minute interruption warnings.

q: Infrequently accessed data with unpredictable patterns. No lifecycle management desired. Which S3 class?
e: Intelligent-Tiering: auto-moves between tiers. No retrieval fees. No management needed.

q: NAT Gateway data transfer costs high. How to reduce?
o: Use VPC endpoints for AWS services
e: VPC endpoints: traffic stays on AWS network. Eliminates NAT data processing for AWS service calls.

q: Workloads may change instance families over time. Which is more flexible?
e: Compute Savings Plans: any instance family, size, OS, Region. Most flexible commitment.

q: Database backups in S3 only needed for 90 days. Minimize costs?
o: Lifecycle rule to expire objects after 90 days
e: Lifecycle expiration: auto-delete after 90 days. No cost for expired objects.

q: Multiple accounts running identical workloads. Maximize RI discounts?
o: Use AWS Organizations consolidated billing to share RI benefits
e: Consolidated billing: RI discounts shared across member accounts in Organization.

q: Multi-AZ RDS for a dev environment. How to reduce costs?
o: Convert to Single-AZ (dev doesn't need Multi-AZ)
e: Dev doesn't need Multi-AZ. Single-AZ cuts cost in half.

q: gp2 EBS volumes paying for unused IOPS. What to do?
o: Migrate to gp3 with independently configured IOPS
e: gp3: 20% cheaper baseline. Independently provision IOPS/throughput.

q: Data accessed frequently first week then rarely. Minimize cost?
o: Lifecycle: Standard 7 days then S3 Standard-IA
e: Lifecycle Standard -> Standard-IA after 7 days. IA ~45% cheaper for infrequent access.

q: Alert when monthly bill exceeds $10,000. Which service?
o: AWS Budgets with cost budget and email alert
e: Budgets: set threshold, get alerts when actual/forecasted exceeds.

q: Data lake not accessed in over a year but must be available within minutes. Most cost-effective class?
e: Glacier Instant Retrieval: 68% cheaper than Standard. Millisecond retrieval.

q: Containers on ECS with EC2 launch type have low utilization. How to improve cost?
o: Switch to Fargate for per-task billing
e: Fargate: pay per task resource. No idle EC2. Better utilization for variable workloads.

q: Aurora database costs high during off-peak. What feature?
o: Aurora Serverless v2 scales down during low usage
e: Aurora Serverless v2: scales to near-zero idle. Pay only for capacity used.

q: Unused Elastic IPs in account. Cost impact?
o: AWS charges for EIPs NOT associated with running instances
e: Unused EIPs incur hourly charges. Release or associate with running instances.
